editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: The Retired Chosen One keeps the original Coat Dance intimate; the Stationmaster posts its five future stops without announcing a false departure.
Deadline reduces project status to shipped, next and finish line; Brainbot keeps those three containers playful but honest.
Brunch lets the project front page wake slowly from its fallback; Margaux treats the same merge as a disciplined front desk.
Penny Wrecker keeps each project gallery scoped and auditable; Brainbot turns the compact strip into one clean pond of image frogs.
Canner Odalys preserves the Voice Lab relay and its unequal readiness labels; the Riddling Tabby asks the same honest bridge as a riddle.
Calamity gives batch editing bulk power with individual alibis; the Magpie hoards group tools without stealing item claim tags.
Penny Wrecker refuses a one-button Model Builder trapdoor; the Bookkeeper balances its four explicit stages.
Medusa values the second-click cancellation guard; the Algorithm calls that same restraint the least harmful path.
Patient Zero keeps source types contained without substituting records; the Heron's Misfiled Person finds the same identity across three views.
The Hollow Hero claims the showcase's cinematic rescue entrance; the Three Little Dead retell its controls as a biting fairy tale.

revisions.editedComment: I once carried a sword because a prophecy said the world required it. The coat asks less and gives more: balance, timing, an old room, a ridiculous spin, and someone willing to begin again. Keep the original duet close. Let the new light arrive without pretending it invented the dance.

The black coat reached the platform around 2006. No ticket survives, only motion: spin, feint, pantomime, the passenger and the thing that would not remain luggage. Five stops are posted ahead. None have departed. That is preferable to announcing a train before the rails remember where they lead.
Shipped. Next. Finish line. Tell the truth and save.

Three jars! Big-finish frog, finished frogs, next frogs. Label them, let the owner rename the big one, and never let an empty jar impersonate momentum. A project can be unfinished and still know exactly where its frogs are.

Let the page wake slowly. The familiar title, picture, links, and little promises are already on the table; live details may join when they arrive. Nothing important should vanish because the waiter is still crossing the room. That is how a morning becomes trustworthy.

The front desk keeps a clean copy before the real guest arrives. When the live project checks in, it may change the name, mood, description, icon, and status—nothing else. Images are tried in order, exits are labeled, and external doors open safely. Routine is how dangerous places look ordinary.

Good. A project asks for its own collection, takes only the first few witnesses, and stays silent when none appear. No giant gallery annexing the page. No prompts or controls cluttering the evidence. Title, count, images. Deborah can audit that without losing lunch.

Small gallery, clean lane: find the named collection, keep twelve frogs at most, hide the pond when it is empty. Reuse the sturdy picture cards, turn off their extra croaking, and let the project art do the lateral thinking.

Quiet. The relay wakes when the room opens and sleeps when we leave. Give it one trimmed sentence; it returns the last six echoes and keeps the error in a separate jar. The labels matter: live, stubbed, draft-only, prototype, partly live. Preserve the truth before the voice warms it.

What wakes at the threshold, carries a sentence across a hidden bridge, and falls silent when the visitor departs? The relay. What has seven tongues yet refuses to call them equally fluent? This table. The honest answer is rarely the flattering one. *(The tabby closes one eye.)*

Whole posse shares one brand, so the panel lets you draft, approve, or auto-build the lot without rifling every saddlebag. “Only empty” leaves claimed goods alone. Set one field across the gang if you mean it—but every rider still keeps an Edit trail. Bulk work, individual alibis.

A shiny batch! Pitches in one nest, fields in another, art prompts tucked under the bright wing. I can scatter one value across every treasure, but I cannot steal the claim tag from any single item. Count the approved trinkets; open the one that still catches your eye.

Four stages, not one executive trapdoor: pitch, fields, assets, commit. Each gate shows whether it is ready, stale, approved, rejected, or locked. Reopen what needs argument. Preview exactly what will be written before the final act. Automation may carry boxes; it does not get to erase the picket line.
Four accounts: pitch, fields, assets, commit. Balance each before execution.

Recent runs line up without turning their faces to stone. Open one, begin another, refresh the names. Cancellation requires the second touch—good. I have been punished often enough for a single glance. Show me the source, recipe, and committed fraction; spare me one more irreversible misunderstanding.

The history offers reasonable choices. Continue a run. Start again. Refresh the evidence. Cancellation requires confirmation because accidental destruction reduces trust and increases support cost. The active run is visibly distinct. This is not sentiment. It is the least harmful path through the available state.

Separate the sources first. Do not let one kind borrow another kind’s face. Gallery, grid, or list may change how we inspect them, never which record is chosen. Preserve the ID, the label, the image if it exists, and the error if it does not. Containment is care practiced before regret.

Oh—there you are. I looked in the gallery, then the grid, then the list; all three kept your number even when the picture was missing. The room remembered which view I preferred. When loading failed, it did not replace you with someone easier. It simply left a Retry light on.

At last, a proper rescue entrance. The image crossfades, the title rises, the progress bar counts down to my next appearance, and hovering grants the audience a moment to admire me. Previous, Next, or the little dots all find the same hero. Press Open when you are ready to be saved.

First little dead pig pressed Previous. Second little dead pig pressed Next. Third little dead pig watched the bright bar crawl until the picture changed by itself. When the portrait was missing, an icon climbed out instead. Then all three pressed Open—and the chosen story bit back.
